0.body: VioletScript is a robust statically-typed scripting language with syntax resembling EcmaScript.
0.title: Language Overview

1.body: This section shows example VioletScript programs.
1.title: Examples

10.body: VioletScript supports proxies to alter the behavior of certain operations. Currently, only class and enum can define proxies; interface cannot define proxies.
10.title: Proxies

100.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Virtual Properties » Virtual Properties
100.title: Virtual Properties

101.title: Functions

105.body: Array destructuring syntax can in addition be used for tuples. [x, y] = someArray;
105.title: Array Destructuring

107.body: A decorator is a function that either returns void or returns another function that returns void, applied to either a type, property or method as expressions enclosed by square brackets. Decorators cannot be applied everywhere. [D(x = 10)] is equivalent to [D({x: 10})].
107.title: Decorators

108.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Decorators » Decorators Applied to Types
108.title: Decorators Applied to Types

109.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Decorators » Decorators Applied to Properties
109.title: Decorators Applied to Properties

11.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Proxies » Conversion Proxies
11.title: Conversion Proxies

110.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Decorators » Decorators Applied to Methods
110.title: Decorators Applied to Methods

111.body: The following decorators are reserved, but can still be used if they do not directly appear as lexical references: When applied to a class definition, [Value] is reserved When applied to an enum definition, [Flags] is reserved
111.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Decorators » Reserved Lexical Decorators
111.title: Reserved Lexical Decorators

112.body: This section is in construction. For information on built-in objects, refer to github.com/violetscript/ls .
112.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Built-in Objects » Built-in Objects
112.title: Built-in Objects

114.body: This document explains how VioletScript should be implemented in VioletScript.
114.title: Bootstrapping

115.body: com.violetscript.parser.source.Span, a Value class, should contain: script:Script start:StringIndex end:StringIndex firstLine:Int lastLine:Int The following are derived from the script using the fields above: firstColumn:Int zero based first column firstLineStartUTF8 = span.script.getLineStartUTF8(span.firstLine) firstColumn = UTF8.utf8IndexToIndex(span.script.source, span.start.utf8, {initialIndexUTF8: firstLineStartUTF8, initialIndex: 0}) lastColumn:Int zero based last column lastLineStartUTF8 = span.script.getLineStartUTF8(span.lastLine) lastColumn = UTF8.utf8IndexToIndex(span.script.source, span.end.utf8, {initialIndexUTF8: lastLineStartUTF8, initialIndex: 0}) com.violetscript.parser.tokenizer.TokenData should contain similiar fields.
115.breadcrumbs: Compiler Guide » Bootstrapping » Span and TokenData
115.title: Span and TokenData

116.breadcrumbs: Compiler Guide » Including and Excluding Sources » Including and Excluding Sources
116.title: Including and Excluding Sources

117.body: When using the include directive to include another source, make sure that source is not included in the compiler options, otherwise it will cause source conflict.
117.breadcrumbs: Compiler Guide » Including and Excluding Sources » Use of include directive
117.title: Use of include directive

13.title: Binary Operator Proxies

14.body:
These proxies enable use of the literal this. class C { // o[i]; proxy function getIndex(i:I):V ( ... ); // o[i] = v; proxy function setIndex(i:I, v:V) { ... } // delete o[i]; proxy function deleteIndex(i):Boolean { ... } // v in o; proxy function has(v):Boolean ( ... );
}
14.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Proxies » Index Proxies
14.title: Index Proxies

15.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Proxies » Iteration Proxies
15.title: Iteration Proxies

16.title: Type Conversions

17.title: Conversion Syntax

18.body: The dynamic type (*) includes values of all types.
18.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Any Type » Any Type
18.title: Any Type

19.body: The undefined type includes the value undefined .
19.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Undefined Type » Undefined Type
19.title: Undefined Type

2.breadcrumbs: Language Overview » Examples » Hello World » Hello World
2.title: Hello World

20.body: The null type includes the null value.
20.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Null Type » Null Type
20.title: Null Type

21.body: The Boolean type includes the values false and true.
21.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Boolean Type » Boolean Type
21.title: Boolean Type

22.body: There are different numeric types: type DoublePrecisionFloatingPoint = Number; type QuadruplePrecisionFloatingPoint = Decimal; type Unsigned8BitInteger = Byte; type Signed16BitInteger = Short; type Signed32BitInteger = Int; type Signed64BitInteger = Long; type ArbitraryPrecisionInteger = BigInt;
22.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Number Type » Number Type
22.title: Number Type

23.body: The String type is UTF-8 encoded.
23.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » String Type » String Type
23.title: String Type

24.body: Integer indexes are dynamically converted into octet indexes: '\u{10000}foo\u{10000}'.substr(1, 3) == 'foo' The following returns a StringIndex object: 'violetscript'.indexOf('let') StringIndex indexes are always efficient: '\u{10000}foo\u{10000}'.substr(stringIndexObject, 3)
24.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » String Type » Code Points
24.title: Code Points

25.body: The String type can be additionally indexed with a GraphemeIndex object.
25.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » String Type » Unicode Graphemes
25.title: Unicode Graphemes

26.title: Enumeration Types

27.title: Custom Properties

28.title: Type Inference

29.title: Flags

30.title: Custom Numeric Type

32.title: Interfaces

33.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » User Value Types » User Value Types
33.title: User Value Types

34.body: The Array type is dynamically-sized. The following line: type ListOfNumbers = [Number]; is equivalent to: type ListOfNumbers = Array.<Number>;
34.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Array Types » Array Types
34.title: Array Types

35.body: Tuple types are passed by value. They are written as two or more type expressions enclosed by square brackets. For example: type T = [Number, String];
35.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Tuple Types » Tuple Types
35.title: Tuple Types

36.body: Record types are objects passed by value. They are written in curly brackets. For example: type R = {x:Number, y:undefined|RegExp};
36.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Record Types » Record Types
36.title: Record Types

37.body:
Function types inherit the Function class. // parameterless
type F1 = ()->void; // with required parameter
type F2 = (a:Number)->void; // with optional parameter
type F3 = (a?:Number)->void; // with rest parameter
type F4 = (...a:[Number])->void;
37.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Function Types » Function Types
37.title: Function Types

38.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Union Types » Union Types
38.title: Union Types

39.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Type Parameters » Type Parameters
39.title: Type Parameters

4.breadcrumbs: Language Overview » Examples » Iteration » Basic Range Loops
4.title: Basic Range Loops

40.body: Use the context keyword type for defining type aliases. type AliasName = q.f.C; type G.<T> = g.C.<T>;
40.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Type Aliasing » Type Aliasing
40.title: Type Aliasing

41.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Types » Static Properties » Static Properties
41.title: Static Properties

43.body:
The context keyword embed can be used to include text or octet-stream of relative path to the current script. var ba:ByteArray = embed './octetStream.bin';
var str:String = embed './someText.txt';
43.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Embed Expression » Embed Expression
43.title: Embed Expression

44.body: The await expression causes the surrounding function to be asynchronous, returning a Promise. It can also appear in the top-level of a program.
44.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Await Expression » Await Expression
44.title: Await Expression

45.body: The yield expression causes the surrounding function to be a generator, returning Generator.
45.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Yield Expression » Yield Expression
45.title: Yield Expression

46.body: The default expression returns the default value of the given type. default(Number); // 0
46.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Default Expression » Default Expression
46.title: Default Expression

47.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Function Expression » Function Expression
47.title: Function Expression

48.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Markup Expression » Markup Expression
48.title: Markup Expression

49.body: Object initializer, written in curly brackets, supports shorthand property notation, trailling comma, spread components (...spreadObject) and a suffix type annotation. Object initializer can only initialize: Map Flags enumeration Record type Class without constructor and without inherited fields
49.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Object Initializer » Object Initializer
49.title: Object Initializer

5.body: This section lists all language features of VioletScript, avoiding grammar and implementation details.
5.title: Language Reference

50.body: Array initializer, written in square brackets, supports ellisions, spread components (...otherArray) and suffix type annotation. Array initializer can only initialize: Array Set Flags enumerations Tuples A spread component can either be a compatible Array or iterator.
50.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Array Initializer » Array Initializer
50.title: Array Initializer

51.body:
o.x; // optional member access in case o is undefined or null
o?.x; If o is a package, then o.x can resolve to a subpackage in last attempt.
51.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Member Expression » Member Expression
51.title: Member Expression

52.body:
Unlike EcmaScript, the index expression, in the form o[k], uses an indexing mechanism that is separate from the object properties mechanism. This resolves conflict of properties and indexes. Methods such as Reflect.get(...) can be used in case a property must be dynamically resolved. The index expression is indentation-aware. o[k]; // optional index in case o is undefined or null
o?.[k];
52.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Index Expression » Index Expression
52.title: Index Expression

53.body:
Call expression is indentation-aware. f(); // optional call in case f is undefined or null
f?.();
53.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Call Expression » Call Expression
53.title: Call Expression

54.body: If f is an enumeration, the call expression corresponds to a forced conversion from either String or numeric value to the enumeration. If f is a type, but not an enumeration, it corresponds to the use of the new operator.
54.breadcrumbs: Language Reference » Expressions » Call Expression » Special Behavior
54.title: Special Behavior

delete r[k]
typeof v
void v
!v
+v
-v
~v
v!
++v
--v
v++
v--
new C

64.body: The increment and decrement operators can also be applied to expressions in the form x!, such as o[k]!++;.

64.title: Increment/Decrement Semantics

65.body:
k in o;
x + y
x - y
x * y
x / y
x % y // remainder
x ** y
x && y
x ^^ y
x || y
x ?? y
x & y
x ^ y
x | y
x << y
x >> y
x >>> y
x == y
x != y
x === y
x !== y
x < y
x > y
x <= y
x >= y
nonDestructuringPattern = y
destructuringPattern = y
nonDestructuringPattern compound= y

7.body: The following line aliases the dynamic type (*): type UndefinedOrNullOrObject = *; The following line aliases the Object type: type ObjectNotUndefinedNorNull = Object; The following line escapes o from either null or undefined: o! The following line escapes T from union with null and undefined: type NonNullT = T!;
7.title: Nullability
